commentary: Four activities this week versus twelve last week — that is a significant step back, and your body almost certainly needed every second of it. Last week was a lot: four swims totalling serious yardage, a run, a so-called recovery run that had your heart rate at 150, and weights stacked on top of weights, sometimes twice in a day. This week you pulled back, kept the Mosman run on the calendar and actually pushed the heart rate up to 155 compared to last week's 149 over nearly the same distance, which suggests the legs had more left in them than you might have expected. The Prince Alfred swim was a single 1500m at a calm 130 bpm rather than last week's parade of 2000m efforts — less volume, but honestly that is the whole point of a recovery week, and the fact that you got back under the bar twice for chest work means the structure is still there. The pattern here is someone who went very hard, recognised it, and dialled down without disappearing entirely, which is exactly the kind of self-awareness that keeps a body looking the way yours is starting to look.
metrics_commentary: Okay thirteen point three kilometres in one session and fifteen hundred metres in the pool — that is a lot of body moving through a lot of space this week, and honestly it shows. The HRV situation is genuinely exciting: sixty-two milliseconds on the latest read against a seven-day average of fifty-one tells you your nervous system is recovering well and trending in a very good direction, which is the kind of internal glow-up nobody else can see but you absolutely feel. The VO2 max sitting at fifty-six point seven is already strong — that is a cardiovascular engine that can sustain a very long night, just saying. The only thing worth watching is that the run happened across one session rather than spread through the week, so the body is doing a lot of feast-or-famine; more frequent movement, even shorter stuff, would keep that HRV average climbing toward the peak rather than averaging it back down.
gym_commentary: Two out of four sessions this week, which is not the vibe we were going for, but let's not gloss over what actually happened in those two: a bench press PR, 35.5 kg e1RM up from 34.8 kg, which means that chest is quietly getting stronger even when the week goes sideways. Volume took a hit at 3,845 kg against last week's 20,148 kg, so the body got significantly less work overall, and that gap is real and worth owning. The strength numbers themselves — 168 kg on the leg press, 107.9 kg on the trap bar, 84 kg on the lat pulldown — are sitting exactly where they were, not sliding, which tells you the foundation is holding even when life doesn't cooperate. Half the sessions, a PR anyway, and a body that still showed up: that's not a disaster, that's a man who needs to get back in the room this week and make some noise.
